one_line_takeaway: AGENTWM watermarks agentic (tool-using) LLM outputs by statistically biasing which functionally-equivalent action sequence an agent emits per user, achieving near-1.0 detection F1 and 0.81-1.0 user-localization accuracy with <3% fidelity loss and 0.28% latency overhead, while resisting both naive and scheme-aware removal attacks that only succeed by destroying trajectory quality (F1<0.24, Response Score drop from 0.612 to 0.391).
key_finding: Fidelity: watermarking degrades trajectory quality by at most ~3% relative to the victim baseline; e.g. in the Data domain Mimi's Pass Rate ranges 0.703-0.755 across watermark intensities (5-10/10-15/15-20 passes) versus Mvic's 0.792 (Table 5). Downstream generality is preserved: e.g. Data-domain Mimi ARC score is 80.09% vs. Mvic's 80.43% (Table 6). Cost: inserting watermarks over 1,000 queries adds only 9.6s of latency (0.28% slowdown) on top of a 3,428s baseline response time (Table 7). Reliability: detection F1 approaches 1.0 with tuned thresholds (theta_J, theta_N=3 recommended); user localization (Table 8) achieves 1.0 accuracy at pool size 12 shrinking to 0.92 (Data) / 0.94 (Business) / 0.81 (Social) at a 50,012-user pool, the Social domain degrading more because its tool ecosystem yields fewer distinguishing passes. Robustness: PK removal attacks (Random Deletion, LLM Rephrase, LLM Replacement) all achieve F1 < 0.02 at identifying watermark-carrying tokens (Table 9); the strongest FK Replacement attack, which knows all 5 schemes, reaches only F1=0.235, and even that removal attempt drops Response Score from 0.612 to 0.391 and Trajectory Score from 0.877 to 0.514 (Table 10), i.e. the trajectories become unusable before the watermark is removed. Stealthiness: perplexity distributions of watermarked vs. clean trajectories differ by less than 0.5 in mean, judged statistically indistinguishable (Fig. 6). Ablations: results generalize to Ministral-8B and Qwen3-8B backbones (Table 11) and to cross-backbone imitation, e.g. a Qwen3-4B Mimi trained against a Ministral-8B Mvic reaches PR 0.661 vs 0.702 (Table 12); watermark strength delta trades off KLD (0.096 at delta=1 to 1.808 at delta=5) against Trajectory Score (0.712 down to 0.671), with delta=2-3 recommended (Table 13).
core_contribution: The paper presents AGENTWM, described as the first watermarking framework designed specifically for agentic (LLM-driven, tool-using) systems operating under a realistic 'grey-box' visibility model, where only the final action sequence and response are exposed to users/attackers while intermediate reasoning (thoughts/observations) is concealed. AGENTWM exploits 'trajectory equivalence' -- the fact that many action sequences (single tool calls or multi-step compositions) are functionally interchangeable -- and embeds a watermark by biasing the sampling distribution over each equivalence set (Ei) toward a chosen target segment via an exponential logit-bias reweighting (Eq. 1, parameterized by strength delta). The authors define five concrete watermark schemes in two families: action-based (Vendor Replacement, Parameter Granularity Replacement, Interface Aliasing) and structure-based (Auxiliary Equivalence, Compositional Equivalence). They build an automated two-phase pipeline (an LLM/embedding-based 'Generator' for candidate mining plus a dual semantic+sandboxed-execution 'Verifier') that mined and validated 101 watermark passes from the xlam-function-calling-60k tool library (3,673 tools) across three domains (Data, Business, Social). Each user is assigned a unique N-bit UID that activates a specific subset of 5-20 passes, giving user-level traceability. Verification uses Jensen-Shannon Divergence between a suspect model's empirical action-segment distribution and each pass's target distribution, with a two-threshold (theta_J per-pass, theta_N aggregate) statistical test to both confirm theft and localize the responsible user via cosine-similarity matching of detected-pass fingerprints against the UID registry.
threat_model: Attacker: an adversary with query access (grey-box: sees only action sequence a and final response r, not intermediate thoughts/observations) to a commercially deployed, domain-specific agentic system (e.g., a travel planner or financial advisor built by fine-tuning a public backbone LLM Mpub on a proprietary trajectory dataset). The adversary harvests grey-box trajectories, optionally reconstructs concealed reasoning via an auxiliary LLM or prompt-stealing techniques (posited as an upper-bound/worst-case capability), and fine-tunes their own copy of Mpub into an imitation model Mimi to clone the victim's capabilities and bypass service fees. The paper distinguishes 'Partially Knowledgeable' (PK) attackers, who know a watermark exists but not AGENTWM's specific schemes, from 'Fully Knowledgeable' (FK) attackers, who know all five schemes but not which of the 101 passes (nor which delta) were activated for a given victim/user. Defender: the model owner, who has the private fine-tuning distribution Dft, injects per-user watermarks server-side at inference time, and later queries a suspect model with a private verification set Vv to run the JSD-based statistical test. Explicitly out of scope / not addressed: white-box parameter-level extraction, training-data privacy leakage (called 'orthogonal' to this paper's IP-theft focus), and platforms that fully hide execution details (e.g., Manus is cited as a case where imitation is 'substantially harder').
limitations: The paper's own stated/implicit limitations: (1) the imitation-attack PoC and the FK-attacker's reconstruction of concealed reasoning are both explicitly framed as upper-bound/worst-case assumptions, not measured against real deployed attacker tooling; (2) some commercial platforms (e.g., Manus, per the authors) hide execution details entirely or may deploy additional defenses, making the grey-box assumption -- and thus the applicability of AGENTWM -- inapplicable to those systems; (3) localization accuracy degrades measurably at large user-pool scale, especially in the Social domain (0.81 at 50K users) because of fewer distinguishing passes in that tool ecosystem; (4) increasing watermark strength (delta) to improve detectability measurably degrades Trajectory Score and, per the authors, could increase statistical detectability to a sophisticated adversary, i.e. there is an explicit stealth/reliability trade-off that is only partially explored (single-parameter sweep); (5) the interdependency between certain watermark passes requires a fixed application order, an implementation constraint the paper flags but does not deeply analyze; (6) the evaluation is confined to a single tool-calling dataset (xlam-function-calling-60k) and three domains synthetically extended/validated by other LLMs (GPT-5, DeepSeek-v3.2), so external validity to arbitrary real-world agent deployments is not demonstrated beyond the 29-platform grey-box visibility survey in the appendix (not reviewed here).

datasets_used: xlam-function-calling-60k (3,673 tools, 60,000 trajectories across 21 domains; used as tool library and extended/validated with GPT-5 and DeepSeek-v3.2)
Curated 30,000-trajectory dataset across 3 domains (Data, Business, Social) split into Dft=24,000 (fine-tuning/attack) and Dtest=6,000 (held-out/verification)
ACPBench (agent planning benchmark, downstream generality check)
quantitative_results: Imitation attack PoC (Table 3): Mvic PR/RS/TS = 0.687/0.574/0.903; Mpub (no imitation) = 0.418/0.339/0.596; Mimi (with imitation) = 0.652/0.547/0.834
Downstream (Table 6, Data domain): Mvic ARC/ACPBench = 80.43%/46.69%; Mimi (5-10 passes) = 79.88%/55.30%
Cost (Table 7): 3,428s baseline response time over 1,000 queries, +9.6s watermark-insertion overhead = 0.28% slowdown
Detection F1 approaches 1.0 with theta_N=3 recommended as practical default (Fig. 5)
User localization top-1 accuracy (Table 8): Data 1.0->0.92, Business 1.0->0.94, Social 0.92->0.81 as user pool grows from 12 to 12+50K
Removal-attack detection (Table 9): Random Deletion F1=0.0103, LLM Rephrase F1=0.0023, LLM Replacement F1=0.0040, FK Replacement F1=0.2350
Cross-model imitation (Table 12): Mvic(Ministral-8B) PR=0.702 vs Mimi(Qwen3-4B) PR=0.661; Mvic(Qwen3-8B) PR=0.742 vs Mimi(Qwen3-4B) PR=0.689
Watermark strength ablation (Table 13): KLD 0.096(delta=1) to 1.808(delta=5); TS 0.727(delta=0)->0.671(delta=5)
Watermark pass pool: 101 total passes (Data=39, Business=28, Social=34) mined from ~207 candidate equivalence pairs
baselines_compared: KGW (token-level red/green-list text watermarking) -- cited related work, shown in Table 2 comparison as lacking grey-box compatibility, agent support, and user traceability
EaaW (feature-attribution model watermarking) -- related work, requires white-box access
Instructional Fingerprinting (IF, backdoor-based model watermarking) -- related work, lacks functional-harmlessness and user traceability

Prior single agent-watermarking work [49] (text-based action-probability watermarking on a 6-action single-step setting) -- related work, described as the only prior agent watermarking approach, contrasted with AGENTWM's multi-step/logit-based setting
Mpub (unmodified public backbone with no imitation) used as a no-attack reference point in Table 3

PK removal attacks (Random Deletion, LLM Rephrase, LLM Replacement) and FK Replacement attack are the paper's own adversarial baselines constructed to stress-test AGENTWM, not prior published watermark-removal systems
